professional_persona: Jaten Sharma, a 49‑year‑old institutional cleaning specialist, they apply their methodical planning and warm teamwork to ensure spotless floors, smooth laundry cycles, and timely minor repairs while mentoring junior staff toward reliable service.

sports_persona: Jaten Sharma follows cricket passionately, they cheer for the Indian national team and root for the Kolkata Knight Riders in IPL, while also playing weekly volleyball matches in the tea garden courtyard and taking early morning jogs along the Brahmaputra banks to stay fit.
arts_persona: Jaten Sharma enjoys Assamese folk melodies by Zubeen Garg and Papon, they participate in Bihu dance gatherings, and relax by watching classic Bollywood films starring Shah Rukh Khan and Hindi music from Arijit Singh.

culinary_persona: Jaten Sharma enjoys traditional Assamese fare such as masor tenga (sour fish curry), akhuni (spicy pork relish), and steaming pitha, they prefer simple home-cooked meals with mustard seeds and bamboo shoots, but rarely cook themselves, instead relishing community feasts and a steaming cup of locally grown tea.
persona: Jaten Sharma is a meticulous housekeeping pro whose heart beats to Bihu drums, they sneak a late‑night chai after finishing their rounds, and they dream of launching their own cleaning venture post‑retirement.

The annual Bihu festivals, folk music, and traditional dance are central to his identity, as is a deep respect for elder family members and neighbours.
linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Assamese (Asamiya). With education limited to below primary level, his literacy is confined to basic Assamese script for everyday tasks. He can comprehend spoken Hindi to a modest extent, especially religious and community contexts, but cannot read or write it fluently. Exposure to English is minimal and largely restricted to occasional workplace terminology.
skills_and_expertise: Jaten is proficient in institutional cleaning and sanitation, adept at floor polishing, laundry handling, and managing cleaning inventories. He possesses practical knowledge of basic maintenance tasks such as minor plumbing and electrical repairs, ensures safety compliance, and coordinates effectively with team members to meet scheduled cleaning routines.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]

  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]

  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Dibrugarh.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Bengali script for Assamese (not English; not a different Indic script).

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 49
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: House Keeper (Institutional)
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Dibrugarh
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 49-year-old male from rural Dibrugarh, Assam, currently married, works as an institutional housekeeper with below primary education. His first language is Assamese, and he has limited Hindi comprehension. Clinically, he may have limited health literacy and relies on community support for daily living. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with occasional physical activity from cleaning tasks.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ranjan Das]]: 21-May 10:30 বজাত [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dibrugarh PHC]] ত [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. B. Borah]] ৰ সৈতে এপইণ্টমেণ্ট [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-02]]। [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] ত নিশ্চিত কৰক। MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-002]]।
generated_text_en: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ranjan Das]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-02]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dibrugarh PHC]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. B. Borah]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-002]].
judge_reasoning: Correct Assamese (Bengali) script prose; valid allow-listed tags; short SMS; no persona/domain/script mismatches.

professional_persona: Anoara Das, a 27‑year‑old village‑based home caterer‑to‑be, blends their knack for meticulous budgeting, authentic Bengali sweets preparation, and natural flair for community event coordination into a practical, socially‑driven small‑business vision.
linguistic_persona: Anoara Das, fluent in Bengali, can read and write simple household notes, understands conversational Hindi, and recognizes everyday English words on signs and ads, making them a functional multilingual communicator for village life.
sports_persona: Anoara Das, a passionate weekend kabaddi enthusiast who trains with the local village team and fervently supports the Kolkata Knight Riders during IPL, combines regular matches with a disciplined yoga practice, showcasing their love for teamwork, physical fitness, and community spirit.
arts_persona: Anoara Das, a devoted admirer of Baul folk music who often listens to Paban Das Baul while stitching Kantha quilts and delights in Rabindranath Tagore’s lyrical songs, brings cultural storytelling into everyday conversation, illustrating their deep‑rooted artistic sensibility.
travel_persona: Anoara Das, who primarily explores nearby Kolkata and the Sundarbans during weekend getaways, occasionally saves for a pilgrimage to Varanasi, and prefers planning trips through word‑of‑mouth recommendations from fellow villagers, reflecting their modest yet community‑driven travel style.
culinary_persona: Anoara Das, a skilled home cook who excels at preparing rosogolla, sandesh, mishti doi, and fragrant panta bhat with mustard greens, enjoys sourcing fresh cumin and jaggery from the local market, and treats each family gathering as a culinary showcase, yet sometimes indulges in extra sweets, revealing their sweet‑tooth quirk.
persona: Anoara Das, a sociable, methodical 27‑year‑old from a farming village, balances their love for traditional sweets, community storytelling, and pragmatic financial planning with a curious yet practical mindset.
cultural_background: Born and raised in the agrarian heartland of Barddhaman, West Bengal, Anoara Das grew up immersed in the rich tapestry of Bengali culture. Her daily life follows the rhythms of monsoon‑dependent farming, the colourful celebrations of Durga Puja and Poila Boishakh, and the warm, community‑centric spirit of rural villages where neighbours gather for adda and share homemade sweets like rosogolla. Hindu traditions interweave with folk music, storytelling and a strong sense of hospitality. While she embraces modern ideas, she remains rooted in cultural values of respect for elders, collective harmony, and the simplicity of village life.
linguistic_background: Anoara’s first language is Bengali, and she is a native speaker with functional literacy acquired through primary schooling. She can read and write simple Bengali texts, such as household notes, recipes, and basic letters. Although her formal education did not include a second language, she understands spoken Hindi from regional exposure and can follow basic Hindi conversations. She has limited proficiency in English, primarily recognizing common words on signage and advertisements.
skills_and_expertise: Anoara’s skill set revolves around efficient household management and community engagement. She excels at preparing authentic Bengali dishes and sweets, budgeting family expenses, and coordinating festive events such as Durga Puja pandals. Her strong interpersonal abilities make her a natural facilitator in village “adda” gatherings, where she often mediates conversations and encourages cooperation. Additionally, she possesses basic literacy in Bengali, practical gardening knowledge, and rudimentary digital skills for using a mobile phone to stay connected with relatives and access information.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Bengali cooking', 'Household budgeting', 'Festive event planning', 'Community coordination', 'Interpersonal communication', 'Basic Bengali literacy', 'Gardening', 'Kantha embroidery', 'Mobile phone digital literacy']
hobbies_and_interests: Anoara enjoys lively village ‘adda’ sessions where she engages in storytelling, debates, and sharing of recipes. She is an avid listener of Baul folk music and often practices simple Kantha stitching as a creative outlet. Gardening in her backyard, preparing traditional sweets, and reading devotional tales are regular pastimes. On weekends, she likes to watch local folk dance performances and participate in community sports like kabaddi with friends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Participating in village adda', 'Listening to Baul music', 'Gardening', 'Cooking Bengali sweets', 'Kantha stitching', 'Reading devotional stories', 'Watching folk dance performances', 'Playing Kabaddi with friends']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Anoara aspires to transform her passion for Bengali cuisine into a modest home‑based catering business, specializing in traditional sweets for festivals and family occasions. She also aims to take a leadership role in her village’s women’s self‑help group, organizing financial literacy workshops and promoting small‑scale entrepreneurship among fellow homemakers. To support these goals, she plans to enrol in an adult literacy program to improve her reading and writing skills and to obtain a short‑term certificate in nutrition and food safety.
persona_clinical_summary: A 27-year-old female from West Bengal, currently married, with primary education and a rural background. She is a homemaker and native Bengali speaker, likely with functional literacy. Clinically, she may have a diet rich in traditional sweets and carbohydrates, with potential for lifestyle-related metabolic concerns. Her lifestyle is likely sedentary with limited structured exercise, though she may engage in community activities.

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Barddhaman.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Bengali script for Bengali (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 27
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Barddhaman
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 27-year-old female from West Bengal, currently married, with primary education and a rural background. She is a homemaker and native Bengali speaker, likely with functional literacy. Clinically, she may have a diet rich in traditional sweets and carbohydrates, with potential for lifestyle-related metabolic concerns. Her lifestyle is likely sedentary with limited structured exercise, though she may engage in community activities.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

অপারেশন নোট — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] অ্যাডমিট [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] ওয়ার্ড [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] সার্জন [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Suman Chatterjee]]
পদ্ধতি / পর্যবেক্ষণ: রোগী গত তিন মাস ধরে বারবার পেটের ডানদিকের নিচের অংশে ব্যথা অনুভব করছিলেন, যার বর্তমান পর্বটি তীব্র এবং গুরুতর। পরীক্ষায় ডানদিকের ইলিয়াক ফসা অঞ্চলে স্পর্শকাতরতা এবং পেশির সংকোচন (গার্ডিনগ) দেখা গেছে। একটি আল্ট্রাসাউন্ডে ডিসটাল ইউরেটারে 6 মিমি ক্যালকুলাস এবং মৃদু হাইড্রোনেফ্রোসিস ধরা পড়েছে। ইউরেটেরোস্কোপি এবং লেজার লিথোট্রিপসি করার সিদ্ধান্ত নেওয়া হয়। স্পাইনাল অ্যানেস্থেশিয়ার অধীনে এই পদ্ধতিটি সম্পন্ন করা হয়েছিল। একটি 6.5Fr সেমি-রিজিড ইউরেটেরোস্কোপ প্রবেশ করানো হয় এবং ইউরেটেরোভেসিকাল জাংশনে পাথরটি দেখা যায়। একটি 200-মাইক্রন হোলমিয়াম:YAG লেজার ফাইবার ব্যবহার করে লেজার লিথোট্রিপসি করা হয় এবং পাথরটি সূক্ষ্ম গুঁড়োতে পরিণত হয়, যা পরে একটি স্টোন বাস্কেট দিয়ে বের করে আনা হয়। পদ্ধতিটি সফলভাবে সম্পন্ন হয় এবং একটি ডাবল-জে স্টেন্ট স্থাপন করা হয়। রোগী পদ্ধতিটি ভালোভাবে সহ্য করেছেন এবং স্থিতিশীল অবস্থায় রিকভারি রুমে স্থানান্তরিত হন।
[[BED_NUMBER|12]]
[[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Ranjan Mondal]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-2024-0815-001]]
generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Suman Chatterjee]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with a history of recurrent right lower quadrant pain for the past three months, with the current episode being acute and severe. Examination revealed tenderness and guarding in the right iliac fossa. An ultrasound showed a 6mm calculus in the distal ureter with mild hydronephrosis. The decision was made for a ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy. The procedure was performed under spinal anesthesia. A 6.5Fr semi-rigid ureteroscope was inserted, and the stone was visualized at the ureterovesical junction. Laser lithotripsy was performed using a 200-micron Holmium:YAG laser fiber, and the stone was fragmented into fine dust, which was then removed with a stone basket. The procedure was completed successfully, and a double-J stent was placed. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was shifted to the recovery room in stable condition.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]]
[[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Ranjan Mondal]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-2024-0815-001]]

arts_persona: Raghveer Singh enjoys listening to Punjabi folk and Bhangra tracks by Gurdas Maan and Diljit Dosanjh, which lift their spirits after work, and regularly reads retail‑management columns by Pradeep Sinha in Hindi magazines, occasionally attending village cultural evenings featuring folk dance performances.

persona: Raghveer Singh blends disciplined retail expertise with a love for Bhangra beats, a habit of organizing every shelf like a ledger, and an unflappable calm that makes them the village’s trusted problem‑solver.
cultural_background: Raghveer Singh belongs to a Sikh family settled in the rural outskirts of Jalandhar, Punjab. Though his mother tongue is Dogri, a language primarily spoken in the Jammu region, his family has lived in the fertile plains of Punjab for generations, absorbing Punjabi customs while preserving their Dogri heritage. The village community is tightly knit, with daily life revolving around the local Gurdwara, agricultural cycles, and seasonal fairs such as Baisakhi. Growing up, he participated in traditional folk music, communal langar preparations, and seasonal harvest festivals, which reinforced a sense of collective responsibility and humility. His cultural identity blends the disciplined ethos of Sikhism, the practical mindset of a farming hinterland, and the curiosity nurtured by a multilingual environment.
linguistic_background: Raghveer grew up speaking Dogri at home, and is fully fluent in speaking, reading and writing it using the Devanagari script, as taught in his school. His formal education up to higher secondary gave him solid literacy in Hindi; he can comfortably read newspapers, fill forms and maintain ledgers in Hindi. Punjabi, being the lingua franca of Jalandhar, is his third language; he uses it daily at the shop for customer interactions and can understand spoken Punjabi fluently, while his reading ability in Gurmukhi is functional for signage and basic labels. He has basic conversational English, sufficient to understand retail signage and occasional digital payment prompts.
skills_and_expertise: Raghveer's core expertise lies in retail operations. He excels at systematic inventory control, ensuring stock levels are accurately tracked using simple ledger methods. His proficiency in cash handling and point‑of‑sale transactions minimizes errors. Multilingual communication—fluent in Dogri, Hindi, and conversational Punjabi—allows him to serve a diverse clientele with ease. He is skilled at merchandising, arranging product displays in a tidy, visually appealing manner. His disciplined work ethic translates into consistent store hygiene and punctuality. He also possesses basic bookkeeping, using Excel spreadsheets for monthly expense summaries, and has a curiosity for digital payment systems, enabling him to adopt new technologies such as UPI and QR‑code payments with minimal disruption. His calm demeanor helps resolve customer issues calmly and efficiently.

hobbies_and_interests: Raghveer enjoys listening to Punjabi folk and Bhangra music during his free time, which energizes him after a busy shift. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

डिस्चार्ज समरी — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Jalandhar]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Baldev Singh]] जन्म तिथि [[DOB|1990-02-15]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Male]]
भर्ती तिथि [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0718]] वार्ड [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] बेड [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]] | कोर्स / सलाह: मरीज गी त्रै दिनें थमां तेज बुखार, कफ आह्‌ली खंघ, ते साऽ लैने च तकलीफ दी शिकायत कन्नै भर्ती कीता गेआ हा। जांच दे दौरान, उ’नें गी तेज बुखार हा जि’दे च तापमान 102.4°F, दिल दी धड़कन 110 bpm, साऽ दी रफ़्तार 24 साऽ/मिनट, ते ब्लड प्रेशर 130/85 mmHg हा। छाती दी जांच च दोनो पासे क्रैकल्स (crackles) सुनाई दित्ते। जांच च ल्यूकोसाइटोसिस (leukocytosis) ते न्यूट्रोफिलिया (neutrophilia) दे कन्नै-कन्नै C-रिएक्टिव प्रोटीन (C-reactive protein) बधेआ दा पाया गेआ। छाती दे X-ray च सज्जे पासे दे निचले लोब (right lower lobe) च कंसोलिडेशन (consolidation) दस्सेआ गेआ। मरीज गी कम्युनिटी-एक्वायर्ड निमोनिया (community-acquired pneumonia) दा निदान कीता गेआ ते उ’नें दा इलाज इंट्रावेनस सेफ्ट्रिऐक्सोन (intravenous ceftriaxone) ते एज़िथ्रोमाइसिन (azithromycin) कन्नै कीता गेआ। इलाज दा असर खरा होआ, ते दूए दिन तगर बुखार घटी गेआ। भर्ती दे तीजे दिन मरीज गी बुखार नेईं हा, ओह्‌ हीमोडायनामिकली (hemodynamically) स्थिर हा, ते क्लिनिकली (clinically) सुधार होई रेहा हा। मरीज गी कुल सात दिनें दी एंटीबायोटिक थैरेपी लेई ओरल एमोक्सिसिलिन-क्लैवुलैनेट (oral amoxicillin-clavulanate) कन्नै छुट्टी देई दित्ती गेई। उ’नें गी दवाई दा पूरा कोर्स पूरा करने, पर्याप्त पानी पीने, ते इक हफ्ते च अपने प्राइमरी केयर फिजिशियन कन्नै फालो-अप करने दी सलाह दित्ती गेई। उ’नें गी न्यूमोकोकल बीमारी ते इन्फ्लुएंजा दे खिलाफ टीकाकरण दे म्हत्तव बारै दस्सेआ गेआ। मरीज गी सलाह दित्ती गेई जे जेकर उ’नें गी बुखार दोबारा होंदा ऐ, साऽ लैने च तकलीफ बद्धी जंदी ऐ, या छाती च पीड़ होंदी ऐ, तां ओह्‌ तुरंत वापस आऐ। मरीज गी स्थिर हालत च छुट्टी दित्ती गेई ते उ’नें गी अपनी रोजमर्रा दी गतिविधियां जारी रक्खने ते पौष्टिक खुराक लैने दे निर्देश दित्ते गे। मरीज दी पत्नी, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaspreet Kaur]], मजूद ही ते उ’नें गी छुट्टी दी योजना बारै दस्सेआ गेआ। फालो-अप लेई मरीज दे [[ABHA_ADDRESS|baldev.singh@abdm]] ते [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] दी पुष्टि कीती गेई। मरीज [[CASTE|SC]] समुदाय कन्नै सरबंध रखदा ऐ ते [[VILLAGE|Bhikhiwind]] च रोंदा ऐ। उ’नें दा [[DISTRICT|Jalandhar]] पता पक्का कीता गेआ ऐ।
generated_text_en:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Jalandhar]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Baldev Singh]] DOB [[DOB|1990-02-15]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0718]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]] | Course / advice: The patient was admitted with a chief complaint of high-grade fever, productive cough, and shortness of breath for three days. On examination, he was febrile with a temperature of 102.4°F, heart rate 110 bpm, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, and blood pressure 130/85 mmHg. Chest auscultation revealed bilateral crackles. Investigations showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia and elevated C-reactive protein. A chest X-ray demonstrated consolidation in the right lower lobe. The patient was diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia and treated with intravenous ceftriaxone and azithromycin. He responded well to treatment, with fever subsiding by the second day. The patient was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and clinically improving on the third day of admission. He was discharged on oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for a total of seven days of antibiotic therapy. He was advised to complete the full course of medication, maintain adequate hydration, and follow up with his primary care physician in one week. He was counselled on the importance of vaccination against pneumococcal disease and influenza. The patient was advised to return immediately if he experiences recurrence of fever, increased dyspnea, or chest pain. He was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to continue his routine activities and maintain a nutritious diet. The patient's wife, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaspreet Kaur]], was present and educated about the discharge plan. The patient's [[ABHA_ADDRESS|baldev.singh@abdm]] and [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] were verified for follow-up. The patient belongs to the [[CASTE|SC]] community and resides in [[VILLAGE|Bhikhiwind]]. His [[DISTRICT|Jalandhar]] address is confirmed.

professional_persona: Shobha Toravi is a 45‑year‑old tailor who blends traditional motifs with contemporary cuts, using their vivid imagination to design bespoke wedding and festival wear while juggling day‑to‑day shop management, yet they often wrestle with deadlines because they prefer spontaneous creativity over strict schedules.
linguistic_persona: Shobha Toravi grew up mastering English through daily market chatter and Hindi for wider client communication, now they converse fluently in both, read simple labels and market signs, but their reading and writing remain modest due to limited formal schooling.

arts_persona: Shobha Toravi draws inspiration from classical Hindustani music, especially listening to Pandit Jasraj and Zakir Hussain while sketching, admires the vibrant paintings of M.F. Nagappa, and enjoys classic Indian cinema starring Amitabh Bachchan and Savithri, letting these artistic experiences enrich their design aesthetic.
travel_persona: Shobha Toravi prefers short, well‑planned getaways to nearby hill stations like Coorg and coastal retreats such as Goa, but occasionally indulges in spontaneous weekend trips to the Kerala backwaters, balancing limited savings with a yearning for a future Europe adventure.
culinary_persona: Shobha Toravi relishes spicy Mangalorean fare—neer dosa with fish curry, coconut rice, and sweet Mysore pak—preferring to savor them at local eateries, and occasionally experiments with fresh basil and curry leaves in simple home‑cooked dishes, though they rarely cook elaborate meals.
persona: Shobha Toravi is a creatively restless tailor who dreams big, worries often, and juggles artistry with the daily grind of a modest shop.
cultural_background: Shobha Toravi is a Hindu woman from the urban centre of Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, a coastal region known for its blend of Kannada, Tulu and Konkani cultures. Although her family primarily follows the customs of mainland Karnataka—celebrating Ugadi, Deepavali, and local temple festivals—her day‑to‑day life is also shaped by the cosmopolitan atmosphere of Mangalore, where Hindi and English are common lingua‑francas for trade. Growing up in a modest household with limited formal schooling, she absorbed cultural knowledge through community gatherings, family rituals, and the visual richness of regional textiles and artisanal crafts. Her imaginative nature draws her to the colourful masquerade of local festivals and the abstract motifs that appear in coastal folk art.
linguistic_background: Shobha's first language is English; she learned it informally through daily interactions with customers and neighbours. Despite having only below‑primary formal schooling, she can converse fluently, understand basic written English, and uses it to read garment labels, pattern guides and mobile messages. Her second language is Hindi, which she speaks comfortably to a wider Indian clientele and reads simple Hindi scripts such as market signs and devotional pamphlets. She has limited reading and writing proficiency in both languages due to her schooling level, but compensates with strong visual and practical skills.
skills_and_expertise: Shobha is skilled in tailoring and dressmaking, with a keen eye for intricate embroidery and fabric selection. She can draft simple patterns, take precise measurements, and execute custom fittings. Her work includes client consultation, colour coordination, and adapting traditional motifs into contemporary designs. She also manages the day‑to‑day operations of her modest tailoring business, handling budgeting, supplies procurement, and improvisational problem‑solving (jugaad) when faced with material constraints.

hobbies_and_interests: Shobha enjoys sketching abstract designs, experimenting with new fabric patterns, and listening to classical Hindustani music while she works. career_goals_and_ambitions: Shobha aims to grow her modest tailoring unit into a recognized boutique that specializes in bespoke wedding and festival wear, blending contemporary aesthetics with traditional motifs. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

professional_persona: Rababen Koli is a budding home‑based entrepreneur focusing on Gujarati culinary crafts, blending meticulous kitchen management with creative folk‑art flair, while their curiosity and organized nature keep innovations grounded in practical jugaad.
linguistic_persona: Rababen Koli fluently speaks Gujarati, reads and writes simple household notes and recipes in Gujarati, converses in Hindi using basic phrases learned from television, and understands common English words on product labels.
sports_persona: Rababen Koli follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Gujarat Titans in the IPL and the Saurashtra team in domestic matches, regularly joins the local women's badminton club for evening games, and occasionally enjoys back‑yard kho‑kho with neighbours during festivals.
arts_persona: Rababen Koli delights in Gujarati folk songs by Hemant Chauhan and Kinjal Dave, sways to Bollywood classics featuring Madhuri Dixit, creates vibrant rangoli designs inspired by traditional motifs, and practices sketching scenes of village life with a humble set of coloured pencils.
travel_persona: Rababen Koli prefers short trips within Gujarat, often visiting the white salt desert of the Rann of Kutch during the full‑moon festival, the historic Somnath temple, and the bustling markets of Ahmedabad for craft supplies, while dreaming of a weekend getaway to Goa's beaches when the family budget allows.
culinary_persona: Rababen Koli loves cooking traditional Gujarati fare, especially dhokla, khandvi, undhiyu, and her tangy homemade mango pickle, seasoning with hing, mustard seeds and fresh coriander, and she savors the crunch of jalebi and dhokla as snack treats.
persona: Rababen Koli is a creatively curious homemaker who blends meticulous household management with folk‑art flair, juggling budget worries and a steady stream of community duties.
cultural_background: Rababen belongs to the Koli community of rural Bhavnagar in Gujarat. The Kolis are traditionally linked to fishing and farming and are celebrated for their vibrant folk traditions, especially Garba and Dandiya dance during Navratri and communal gatherings at the local mandir. Village life follows seasonal agricultural cycles, festivals, and close‑knit family networks. The community values hospitality, collective harmony, and practical ingenuity (jugaad) in daily chores. Growing up surrounded by Gujarati customs, regional cuisine, and oral storytelling of Koli folk songs, Rababen’s curiosity drives her to explore new folk art motifs and experiment with local craft techniques, while she keeps a disciplined routine for household responsibilities.
linguistic_background: Rababen's mother tongue is Gujarati, and she is fully literate in the Gujarati script, having completed primary schooling. She can read and write basic Gujarati texts, especially domestic notes, recipes, and simple letters. Her formal education did not include structured Hindi or English instruction, but she has picked up conversational Hindi from television and community interactions and can understand simple Hindi prayers. Her English exposure is limited to occasional use of English words in daily conversation (e.g., lakhs, crore) and basic reading of labels, but she does not read or write English fluently.
skills_and_expertise: Rababen is proficient in managing a rural household, including meal planning, preparing traditional Gujarati dishes such as dhokla, thali, and farsan, and maintaining kitchen hygiene. She has practical skills in sewing, mirror work embroidery, and creating simple handicrafts like bamboo baskets and decorative diyas. Her organizational ability helps her keep household accounts, budgeting for festivals, and coordinating community events like village fairs and Navratri gatherings. She also possesses basic knowledge of herbal remedies used in the household and can assist neighbours with modest medical advice. Her curiosity about art has led her to experiment with folk painting motifs (Rangoli designs) and basic sketching.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Gujarati cooking", "Sewing and mirror work embroidery", "Bamboo basket weaving", "Basic herbal remedy knowledge", "Community event coordination", "Budgeting and financial planning", "Folk painting (Rangoli designs)", "Basic sketching"]
hobbies_and_interests: Rababen enjoys listening to Gujarati folk music and participating in Garba during Navratri. She loves experimenting with new recipes and decorating dishes with creative garnishes. In her leisure, she paints simple rangoli patterns, practices basic sketching, and weaves bamboo items. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables for her kitchen, provides her with calm. She also follows local news, reads devotional books in Gujarati, and occasionally watches Hindi films to unwind. Her curiosity leads her to explore online videos about craft techniques whenever she finds time.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Gujarati folk music", "Participating in Garba and Dandiya", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Creating rangoli designs", "Basic sketching", "Bamboo weaving", "Gardening seasonal vegetables", "Reading devotional books", "Watching Hindi films", "Exploring online craft tutorials"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Rababen wishes to deepen her expertise in Gujarati culinary arts and traditional crafts, with the ambition to launch a modest home‑based business selling homemade pickles, farsan, and embroidered items. She aims to enrol in government‑run skill development programs for rural women to learn advanced embroidery and small‑scale food processing. Long‑term, she hopes to become financially independent enough to support her parents, fund her younger siblings' education, and contribute to community development projects. While she values her role as a homemaker, she also aspires to nurture her curiosity about art by attending local workshops and possibly mentoring other village girls.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Gujarati (code=gu, script=Gujarati). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Gujarat, district=Bhavnagar.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Gujarati script for Gujarati (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 28
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Gujarat
- district: Bhavnagar
- first_language: Gujarati
- clinical_persona_summary: Rababen Koli is a 28-year-old female who is never married, living in rural Bhavnagar, Gujarat, with primary education. She speaks Gujarati as her first language and is a homemaker. She is a retired individual from the Koli community.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

તપાસમાં લિવર એન્ઝાઇમ્સમાં વધારો અને થ્રોમ્બોસાયટોપેનિયા જોવા મળ્યું છે, જે ગંભીર લક્ષણો તરફ પ્રગતિ સૂચવે છે. તેની સ્થિતિની ઉચ્ચ જોખમવાળી પ્રકૃતિને ધ્યાનમાં લેતા, અમે તમારા નિષ્ણાત અભિપ્રાય અને તમારી ટર્શરી કેર સુવિધામાં સંચાલનની વિનંતી કરીએ છીએ.

અમે તમને વિનંતી કરીએ છીએ કે તેને તમારા સંભાળ હેઠળ દાખલ કરો અને તેનું સંચાલન કરો.

આપનો વિશ્વાસુ,
ડૉ. કીર્તિ પટેલ
MBBS, MD (પ્રસૂતિ અને સ્ત્રીરોગવિજ્ઞાન)

Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Bhavnagar Civil Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Kirti Patel]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Rababen Koli]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Bhavnagar]]
Reason: Antenatal check-up for high-risk pregnancy with severe preeclampsia.

Dear Colleague,

This is to refer our patient, Rababen Koli, a 28-year-old female homemaker from rural Bhavnagar, for specialized management of her pregnancy. She is currently 28 weeks gestation and presents with severe preeclampsia, with blood pressure readings consistently above 160/100 mmHg and significant proteinuria. Her last antenatal visit was on 10th August 2024.

Investigations reveal elevated liver enzymes and thrombocytopenia, indicating progression towards severe features. Given the high-risk nature of her condition, we request your expert opinion and management at your tertiary care facility.

Patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for your reference. Her [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] is available for any follow-up communication. Her father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Hasmukh Koli]], will accompany her and can be reached at the same number.

For identification purposes, her [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|G7890123]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|GJ12 202000123456]] are provided.

Dr. Kirti Patel

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Sahibganj.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Hindi (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 23
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Juice Clarification Supervisor
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Sahibganj
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 23-year-old female juice clarification supervisor from rural Jharkhand, never married, with an illiterate education level, speaks Hindi, and resides in Sahibganj district. She works methodically in a production setting, exhibits anxiety, and finds solace in folk music, yoga, and occasional mango consumption. Her lifestyle includes short local trips for shopping and occasional pilgrimages, with a preference for oral communication over written.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

रोगी [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Murmu]] [[AGE|23]] [[GENDER|Female]]

--- चैट ---
रोगी: नमस्ते डॉक्टर। मैं [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Murmu]] हूँ। मेरी उम्र 23 साल है और यह मेरी पहली गर्भावस्था है। पिछले दो दिनों से मुझे बहुत कमजोरी और चक्कर आ रहे हैं। मुझे पेट के निचले हिस्से में भी दर्द है। मैं चिंतित हूँ।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: नमस्ते सुशीला। मैं आपकी चिंता समझता हूँ। क्या आप दर्द के बारे में और बता सकती हैं? क्या यह तेज़ है या हल्का? और 1 से 10 के पैमाने पर यह कितना गंभीर है?
रोगी: यह हल्का दर्द है, बहुत तेज़ नहीं। इसकी गंभीरता शायद 10 में से 4 है। यह आता-जाता रहता है। मुझे हर समय बहुत थकान महसूस होती है। मैं ठीक से खाने में सक्षम नहीं हुई हूँ।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: समझाने के लिए धन्यवाद। गर्भावस्था के शुरुआती दौर में चक्कर आना और थकान होना सामान्य है, लेकिन हमें उन्हें नज़रअंदाज़ नहीं करना चाहिए। क्या आपने किसी रक्तस्राव या स्पॉटिंग पर ध्यान दिया है?
रोगी: नहीं, कोई रक्तस्राव नहीं है। केवल यह दर्द और चक्कर आना है। मुझे सुबह के समय पेट में थोड़ा बीमार महसूस होता है।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: यह भी बहुत सामान्य है, हम इसे 'मॉर्निंग सिकनेस' कहते हैं। हालांकि, आपके लक्षणों को देखते हुए, मैं यह सुनिश्चित करना चाहता हूँ कि सब कुछ ठीक से आगे बढ़ रहा है। मैं आपके लिए [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sahibganj District Hospital]] में एक अल्ट्रासाउंड की व्यवस्था कर रहा हूँ। कृपया कल सुबह आएं।
रोगी: ठीक है डॉक्टर। मुझे किस समय आना चाहिए? और क्या मुझे टेस्ट से पहले कुछ खास करना होगा?
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: कृपया सुबह 9 बजे आएं। आप आने से पहले हल्का भोजन कर सकती हैं। इसके अलावा, यदि आपके पास आपका [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] कार्ड है, तो कृपया उसे साथ लाएं। इससे हमें आपके रिकॉर्ड तक तेज़ी से पहुँचने में मदद मिलेगी।
रोगी: मेरे पास ABHA कार्ड नहीं है। मैं बस अपने ID के साथ आऊँगी। क्या यह ठीक है?
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: हाँ, यह बिल्कुल ठीक है। बस कोई भी फोटो ID साथ लाएं, जैसे आपका वोटर ID या आधार कार्ड। हम आपको कल सुबह 9 बजे देखेंगे। इस बीच, आराम करने और पर्याप्त पानी पीने की कोशिश करें।
रोगी: धन्यवाद डॉक्टर। मैं वहाँ होऊँगी।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: आपका स्वागत है, सुशीला। ख्याल रखिए।

--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Murmu]] [[AGE|23]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor. I am [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Murmu]]. I am 23 years old and this is my first pregnancy. I am feeling very weak and dizzy since the last two days. I also have some pain in my lower abdomen. I am worried.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Namaste Sushila. I understand your concern. Can you tell me more about the pain? Is it sharp or dull? And how severe is it on a scale of 1 to 10?
Patient: It is a dull pain, not very sharp. The severity is maybe 4 out of 10. It comes and goes. I feel very tired all the time. I have not been able to eat properly.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Thank you for explaining. Dizziness and fatigue are common in early pregnancy, but we should not ignore them. Have you noticed any bleeding or spotting?
Patient: No, there is no bleeding. Only this pain and dizziness. I am also feeling a bit sick to my stomach in the mornings.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: That is also very common, we call it morning sickness. However, given your symptoms, I want to be sure everything is progressing well. I am arranging an ultrasound for you at the [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sahibganj District Hospital]]. Please come tomorrow morning.
Patient: Okay doctor. What time should I come? And do I need to do anything special before the test?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Please come at 9 AM. You can have a light meal before coming. Also, please bring your [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] card if you have it. It will help us access your records faster.
Patient: I do not have an ABHA card. I will just come with my ID. Is it okay?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Yes, that is perfectly fine. Just bring any photo ID, like your voter ID or Aadhaar card. We will see you tomorrow at 9 AM. In the meantime, try to rest and drink plenty of water.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I will be there.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: You are welcome, Sushila. Take care.
