MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]

- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: नमस्कार अञ्जली, सम्पर्क गर्नुभएकोमा धन्यवाद। तपाईँलाई दुखाइ भइरहेको छ भन्ने कुरा मैले बुझेँ। के तपाईँ मलाई दुखाइको बारेमा थप बताउन सक्नुहुन्छ? हिँड्दा वा खोक्दा दुखाइ बढ्छ कि?
बिरामी: हो, डाक्टर। म हिँड्दा वा खोक्दा पनि निकै दुखाइ हुन्छ। मलाई धेरै कमजोरी महसुस भइरहेको छ र म केही पनि राम्ररी खान सकेको छैन।
डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: मैले बुझेँ। तपाईँको लक्षणहरू जस्तै पेटको तल्लो दाहिने भागमा तिखो र स्थानीय दुखाइ, ज्वरो र वान्ताको आधारमा, मलाई एपेन्डिसाइटिस भनिने अवस्थाको चिन्ता छ। यो एक शल्यक्रियात्मक आपत्कालीन अवस्था हो। तपाईँले तुरुन्तै अस्पताल जानुपर्छ।
बिरामी: एपेन्डिसाइटिस? त्यहाँ के हुन्छ? के मलाई शल्यक्रिया आवश्यक पर्छ?
डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: हो, सम्भवतः तपाईँलाई एपेन्डेक्स निकाल्नका लागि शल्यक्रिया आवश्यक पर्नेछ। यो एक धेरै सामान्य र सुरक्षित प्रक्रिया हो। हामीले स्क्यान गरेर निदान पुष्टि गर्नुपर्छ। कृपया तुरुन्तै दिब्रुगढ सिभिल अस्पतालको आपत्कालीन विभागमा जानुहोस्। मसित टेलिमेडिसिन परामर्श भएको जानकारी दिनुहोस्।
बिरामी: ठिक छ डाक्टर। म अहिले जान्छु। बाटोमा हुँदा मैले के गर्नुपर्छ?
डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: कृपया केही पनि नखानुहोस् वा नपिउनुहोस्। शल्यक्रियाका लागि पेट खाली राख्नु महत्त्वपूर्ण छ। यदि तपाईँलाई उच्च ज्वरो छ भने तपाईँले प्यारासिटामोल लिन सक्नुहुन्छ, तर अन्य कुनै पनि दुखाइ कम गर्ने औषधिहरूबाट बच्नुहोस्। सावधानीपूर्वक गाडी चलाउनुहोस् वा कसैलाई तपाईँलाई लैजान भन्नुहोस्। हामी अस्पतालमा तपाईँको लागि तयार हुनेछौँ।
बिरामी: धेरै धेरै धन्यवाद डाक्टर। म हतार गर्छु।
डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: तपाईँलाई स्वागत छ, अञ्जली। हामीले तपाईँको राम्रो हेरचाह गर्नेछौँ। कृपया आफ्नो फोन अन राख्नुहोस्।

Patient: Namaste doctor saheb, my name is Anjali Tamang. I am having severe pain in my lower right abdomen for the last two days. It started as a dull ache but now it is sharp and constant. I also have a fever and I feel like vomiting.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: Hello Anjali, thank you for reaching out. I understand you are in pain. Can you tell me more about the pain? Is it getting worse when you move or cough?
Patient: Yes, doctor. It hurts a lot when I walk or even when I cough. I feel very weak and I have not been able to eat anything properly.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: I see. Based on your symptoms of sharp, localized pain in the lower right abdomen with fever and nausea, I am concerned about a condition called appendicitis. This is a surgical emergency. You need to go to the hospital immediately.
Patient: Appendicitis? What will happen there? Do I need an operation?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: Yes, most likely you will need surgery to remove the appendix. It is a very common and safe procedure. We need to confirm the diagnosis with a scan. Please go to the emergency department of Dibrugarh Civil Hospital right away. Inform them you have a telemedicine consultation with me.
Patient: Okay doctor. I will go now. What should I do while I am on my way?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: Please do not eat or drink anything. It is important to keep your stomach empty for the surgery. You can take paracetamol if you have a high fever, but avoid any other painkillers. Drive carefully or ask someone to take you. We will be ready for you at the hospital.
Patient: Thank you so much, doctor. I will hurry.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Biren Sharma]]: You are welcome, Anjali. We will take good care of you. Please keep your phone on.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Puri.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Odia script for Odia (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 20
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Puri
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 20-year-old female from Puri, Odisha, currently a homemaker with primary education, who speaks Odia as her first language and lives in a rural setting. She is never married and follows a lifestyle centered around traditional arts, community gatherings, and local cultural practices. Clinically, she may have limited exposure to formal healthcare systems due to her rural background and primary education.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Swarna Kanr, a retired village elder and cultural custodian, channels her decades of expertise in traditional household management, Phulkari embroidery, and folk storytelling into mentoring grandchildren, balancing meticulous organization with a vivid imagination and a gentle yet assertive demeanor, and they bring the same disciplined creativity to every community activity they join.
linguistic_persona: Swarna Kanr, a lifelong oral storyteller, speaks fluent Punjabi, understands spoken Hindi in daily conversations, yet cannot read Gurmukhi, Hindi or English scripts, relying on memory and auditory learning for all communication, and they cherish the power of spoken word above written text.
sports_persona: Swarna Kanr enjoys watching Kabaddi matches on TV, follows the Indian Premier League and roots for the Delhi Capitals, takes a leisurely morning walk around the village field, and occasionally joins community Giddha sessions as a lively form of exercise.
arts_persona: Swarna Kanr passionately weaves intricate Phulkari embroidery on cloth, leads spirited Giddha dances during village celebrations, sings devotional bhajans each sunrise, and cherishes listening to legendary folk singers like Surinder Kaur and Jagjit Singh.
travel_persona: Swarna Kanr prefers short pilgrimages to nearby holy sites like Amritsar’s Golden Temple and seasonal trips to Shimla for fresh air, plans trips well in advance with family, travels by train or shared auto, and avoids long‑haul flights due to health concerns.
culinary_persona: Swarna Kanr expertly prepares makki di roti, sarson da saag, and buttery lassi, enjoys the aromatic blend of mustard greens and jaggery, prefers home‑grown vegetables from her kitchen garden, and while she no longer cooks daily, she delights in sharing her recipes during family gatherings.
persona: Swarna Kanr, a vivacious 73‑year‑old keeper of oral tales and Phulkari art, balances frugal living with generous festival splurges, manages health aches with herbal remedies, and radiates curiosity through endless storytelling.
cultural_background: Swarna hails from a small village near Rupnagar in the rural heartland of Punjab. She belongs to a Punjabi Hindu family that has lived in the region for generations, steeped in Punjabi folk traditions, agricultural life, and community festivals such as Baisakhi, Lohri and Teeyan. Her household follows joint‑family customs, with strong emphasis on hospitality, respect for elders and the preservation of oral history.
linguistic_background: Swarna's mother tongue is Punjabi; she speaks Punjabi fluently and understands its nuances, idioms and proverbs. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write Gurmukhi, Hindi or English. She can follow simple spoken Hindi in daily conversations and understands basic instructions from television or neighbours, but she does not read Hindi script. Her linguistic skills are rooted in oral tradition, storytelling and memorised devotional chants.
skills_and_expertise: Swarna excels in managing a Punjabi household, preparing traditional dishes such as makki di roti, sarson da saag, and lassi. She is proficient in Phulkari embroidery, creating intricate needlework for family shawls. She leads Giddha dance performances during festivals, sings devotional bhajans, and narrates folk stories (kisse) to younger relatives. She knows home remedies using local herbs, cultivates a kitchen garden with seasonal vegetables, and mediates family disputes with calm authority.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Punjabi cooking", "Phulkari embroidery", "Giddha dance", "Bhajan singing", "Oral storytelling (kisse)", "Herbal home remedies", "Kitchen gardening", "Family budgeting", "Community dispute mediation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Swarna enjoys listening to Punjabi folk music on the radio, singing bhajans during early mornings, and dancing Giddha at community gatherings. She spends afternoons tending to her kitchen garden, weaving phulkari on small cloth pieces, and recounting folk tales to grandchildren. She also likes watching devotional TV shows and occasionally knitting simple shawls for charity.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Punjabi folk music", "Singing bhajans", "Giddha dancing", "Kitchen gardening", "Phulkari stitching", "Storytelling to grandchildren", "Watching devotional TV shows", "Knitting simple shawls"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Swarna aims to preserve her family’s cultural heritage by teaching her grandchildren Punjabi cooking, Phulkari stitching and folk songs. She wishes to document several family kisse on audio recordings for future generations. She also hopes to stay socially active by volunteering at the village temple’s senior volunteers’ group and learning basic smartphone use to keep in touch with distant relatives.
persona_clinical_summary: A 73-year-old female from rural Punjab, currently married, with no formal occupation and illiterate education. She speaks Punjabi as her first language and follows a traditional lifestyle with limited formal health engagement. Her daily routine includes managing household tasks and occasional herbal remedies for health issues.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Rupnagar.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Gurmukhi script for Punjabi (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 73
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Punjab
- district: Rupnagar
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 73-year-old female from rural Punjab, currently married, with no formal occupation and illiterate education. She speaks Punjabi as her first language and follows a traditional lifestyle with limited formal health engagement. Her daily routine includes managing household tasks and occasional herbal remedies for health issues.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ਰੇਡੀਓਲੋਜੀ ਰਿਪੋਰਟ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|ਗੁਰੂ ਗੋਬਿੰਦ ਸਿੰਘ ਹਸਪਤਾਲ]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|ਜਸਵਿੰਦਰ ਕੌਰ]] [[AGE|73]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|RH-2024-0715-001]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0715-001]] | ਰਿਪੋਰਟ ਡਾ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|ਡਾ. ਗੁਰਚਰਨ ਸਿੰਘ]] ਦੁਆਰਾ ਦਿੱਤੀ ਗਈ
ਨਤੀਜੇ: ਸਿਰ ਦਾ ਨਾਨ-ਕੰਟਰਾਸਟ CT ਸਕੈਨ ਕੀਤਾ ਗਿਆ। ਕੋਈ ਗੰਭੀਰ ਅੰਦਰੂਨੀ ਖੂਨ ਵਹਿਣ, ਮਾਸ ਇਫੈਕਟ, ਜਾਂ ਮਿਡਲਾਈਨ ਸ਼ਿਫਟ ਨਹੀਂ ਮਿਲੀ। ਵੈਂਟ੍ਰੀਕਲਸ ਆਮ ਆਕਾਰ ਦੇ ਹਨ। ਪੈਰਾਨਾਸਲ ਸਾਈਨਸ ਸਾਫ਼ ਹਨ। ਹੱਡੀ ਵਾਲਾ ਕੈਲਵੇਰੀਅਮ ਅਤੇ ਖੋਪੜੀ ਦਾ ਅਧਾਰ ਸਹੀ ਹਨ ਅਤੇ ਕੋਈ ਗੰਭੀਰ ਫ੍ਰੈਕਚਰ ਨਹੀਂ ਹੈ।
ਸਿੱਟਾ: ਨਾਰਮਲ CT ਸਿਰ। ਕੋਈ ਗੰਭੀਰ ਅੰਦਰੂਨੀ ਪੈਥੋਲੋਜੀ ਨਹੀਂ ਹੈ।

[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-PB-045]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

persona: Ahmed Hasan, a 19‑year‑old Sanskrit enthusiast with a penchant for calligraphy and Qawwali, juggles academic dreams and anxiety‑driven yoga sessions, while often forgetting deadlines in favour of spontaneous street‑food cravings.
cultural_background: Ahmed comes from an urban Muslim family in Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, where a deep appreciation for classical Indian culture coexists with Islamic traditions. His household places a strong emphasis on learning Sanskrit as a link to the subcontinent’s literary heritage, while also observing the rituals and community life of the Muslim ummah.
linguistic_background: In terms of language, Ahmed’s mother tongue is Sanskrit, which he reads, writes and speaks fluently thanks to his family’s scholarly tradition and his own schooling up to the 10th standard. He has functional proficiency in English, enough to understand textbooks and communicate in urban settings, while his Hindi skill remains basic, limited to everyday interactions and informal listening.
skills_and_expertise: Ahmed’s expertise lies primarily in classical language studies and related cultural skills. He also possesses basic digital literacy, such as using a smartphone and internet for educational purposes, and is adept at modest home management tasks. His moderate interpersonal communication reflects his balanced social nature, and his emotional reactivity drives him to practice yoga and meditation for self‑regulation.

career_goals_and_ambitions: Ahmed aspires to pursue higher education in Sanskrit literature, aiming for a Bachelor’s degree followed by a Master’s to become a teacher or researcher in a university or cultural institution. He also wishes to develop content that bridges Islamic and Indian classical traditions, possibly through a YouTube channel or community workshops. He hopes to secure a stable job in academia or heritage preservation while managing his emotional well‑being and maintaining flexibility in his schedule.

He resides in an urban setting and speaks Sanskrit as his first language, with functional English proficiency. user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Sanskrit (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Kanpur Nagar
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, with a matriculation education, currently not employed. He resides in an urban setting and speaks Sanskrit as his first language, with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes occasional yoga for anxiety management and a preference for street food, with a culturally Muslim background.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ASHA टिप्पणी — ग्राम [[VILLAGE|शिवपुर]], जनपद [[DISTRICT|कानपुर नगर]]

ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|सुनीता यादव]] | दूरभाष [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
भ्रमण निष्कर्ष: रोगी त्रिसप्ताहं यावत् निरंतरं कासं सूचयति यत्र कदाचित् रात्रौ स्वेदः भवति। सः कदाचित् मन्दं छाती-वेदनां सूचयति तथा च अद्यतनं प्रायः द्विकिलो परिमाणं भारं नष्टवान् अस्ति। तस्य पिता [[RELATIVE_NAME|रमेश खान]], लक्षणानि पुष्टिं करोति तथा च रोगी-अन्न-अतृप्तिं प्रति चिन्तां प्रकटयति। रोगिणः समीपे [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]] तथा [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]] अस्ति। सः [[RELIGION|मुस्लिम]] इति परिचयं करोति। रोगिणं कफ-परीक्षणाय तथा अग्रे मूल्याङ्कनाय जनपद-चिकित्सालयं गन्तुं उपदिष्टम् अस्ति। आगामि-सप्ताहाय अनुवर्ती-भ्रमणं निर्धारितम् अस्ति।

Visit findings: Patient reports persistent cough for three weeks with occasional night sweats. He mentions occasional mild chest discomfort and has lost about two kilograms recently. professional_persona: Jashodaben Makvana, a retired homemaker turned community resource coordinator, applies her methodical budgeting, extensive knowledge of regional cuisine, and organized event‑planning skills to manage family finances and volunteer for temple festivals, while they confidently lead workshops for fellow homemakers.
linguistic_persona: Jashodaben Makvana speaks Sindhi at home, is fluent in Gujarati for everyday interactions, converses comfortably in Hindi, and can read simple texts in both Sindhi and Gujarati scripts, though they have only basic literacy in Hindi.
sports_persona: Jashodaben Makvana stays active by joining the neighbourhood Garba troupe during Navratri, walking briskly in the morning park, avidly following the Gujarat Titans in the IPL and supporting the local cricket team, while they prefer low‑impact exercise and avoid high‑intensity gym routines.
arts_persona: Jashodaben Makvana immerses herself in the arts by listening to Anup Jalota’s bhajans, regional folk tunes and community devotional songs, regularly dancing Garba to the rhythm of local bands, and occasionally attending theatre productions starring Pravin Pandya, while they cherish these cultural expressions as a source of joy and community bonding.
travel_persona: Jashodaben Makvana prefers well‑planned, modest trips, often visiting the Rann of Kutch for the full‑moon festival, taking weekend pilgrimages to Somnath and Dwarka, enjoying annual shopping excursions to Delhi during Diwali, and dreaming of a future family vacation to Dubai’s souks, while they balance travel costs with household budgeting.
culinary_persona: Jashodaben Makvana is a seasoned home cook who expertly prepares regional kadhi, bhindi sabzi, dhokla, khandvi and handvo, delights in making tangy mango and lemon pickles using mustard oil, fenugreek seeds and raw mangoes, and enjoys sharing these dishes at family gatherings, while they occasionally experiment with fusion twists but stay rooted in traditional flavors.
persona: Jashodaben Makvana blends meticulous budgeting, a passion for regional cooking, and a love for lively community festivals into a disciplined yet sociable lifestyle, though they sometimes forget to take their medication on busy days.
cultural_background: Jashodaben comes from a Sindhi Hindu family that settled in Jamnagar, Gujarat after the Partition of India; growing up in the city’s vibrant urban setting, she blends Sindhi traditions such as Cheti Chand celebrations with Gujarati customs like Garba during Navratri, and participates actively in family rituals and community gatherings at the local mandir.
linguistic_background: Jashodaben's mother tongue is Sindhi, which she uses at home and in community events; she is equally fluent in Gujarati for everyday interactions, market visits, and handling local paperwork; with education only below primary, her reading and writing skills are basic, allowing her to read simple texts in both Sindhi and Gujarati scripts and converse easily in Hindi, though she is not formally literate in Hindi.
skills_and_expertise: Jashodaben excels in household management, meticulously planning meals, finances and chores with a methodical approach; she is proficient in cooking a wide array of Sindhi and Gujarati dishes, balancing traditional flavors with the practical needs of her family; her organizational abilities extend to community event coordination, where she volunteers for festivals, Garba gatherings and temple functions; she maintains an accurate family budget despite limited formal education and is adept at basic first‑aid and health care for her household; additionally, her interpersonal skills enable her to mediate minor family disputes and foster cooperation among neighbors.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Sindhi & Gujarati cooking", "Community event coordination", "Family budgeting", "Basic first aid", "Mediation and interpersonal communication"]
hobbies_and_interests: Jashodaben enjoys cooking and sharing traditional Sindhi and Gujarati meals, which lets her combine culinary skill with social interaction. She actively participates in Garba and Dandiya during Navratri, finding joy in dance and community spirit. She tends a small courtyard garden, growing herbs and flowers used in cooking. She loves listening to bhajans and folk songs on the radio and attending temple discourses to deepen her religious knowledge. Additionally, she knits scarves and stitches simple garments for her grandchildren and sometimes joins local women’s devotional book clubs.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cooking traditional Sindhi and Gujarati meals", "Participating in Garba and Dandiya", "Gardening and herb cultivation", "Listening to bhajans and folk songs", "Attending temple discourses", "Knitting scarves and stitching simple garments", "Joining women’s devotional book clubs"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although Jashodaben has retired from formal employment, she wishes to deepen her expertise in traditional Sindhi‑Gujarati cooking, possibly by compiling a small family recipe booklet. She aims to take a more active role in the local women's forum, organising workshops on budgeting and basic health practices for other homemakers. Over the next few years she plans to launch a modest home‑based venture, such as preparing and selling pickles and snacks during local festivals, thereby supplementing the family income while preserving culinary heritage.
persona_clinical_summary: Jashodaben Makvana, a 46-year-old female, is a retired homemaker from Jamnagar, Gujarat, who is currently married and lives in an urban zone. Her first language is Sindhi, with fluency in Gujarati for daily interactions, and she has below-primary education. She manages household finances meticulously and volunteers for community temple events, though she occasionally forgets medication on busy days. Her lifestyle includes regular morning walks and participation in Garba during Navratri, with a diet rich in regional dishes like kadhi and pickles.

طريقہ / مشاهدا: مريضه پيٽ ۾ ڦوڪون (uterine fibroids) سبب مھيني وارو رت جو وھڪرو (menorrhagia) ۽ پيٽ ۾ دٻاءُ محسوس ڪيو. آپريشن کان اڳ جو جائزو الٽراساؤنڊ (ultrasound) ۽ MRI ذريعي ورتو ويو. آپريشن دوران معلوم ٿيو تہ پيٽ جي پٺي واري ڀت (posterior uterine wall) تي 12 cm جي ڦوڪ (fibroid) موجود هئي. مڪمل پيٽ جو آپريشن (total abdominal hysterectomy) سان گڏ دٻن (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy) جو طريقہ انجام ڏنو ويو. رت جو ضياع (blood loss) گھٽ ۾ گھٽ थियो. پيٽ جا نلڪا (ureters) سڃاڻي محفوظ رکيا ويا. نمونو پاٿولوجي (histopathology) لاء موڪليو ويو. آپريشن کان پوءِ مريضه جو حال مستحکم (stable) آهي. مريضه کي مشاهدي (observation) لاء [[BED_NUMBER|12]] ۾ [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]] موڪليو ويو. [[PATIENT_NAME|Jashodaben Makvana]] [[AGE|46]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with symptomatic uterine fibroids causing menorrhagia and pelvic pressure. Preoperative evaluation included ultrasound and MRI. Intraoperative findings revealed a 12 cm intramural fibroid on the posterior uterine wall. Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy was performed. Estimated blood loss was minimal. Ureters were identified and preserved. The specimen was sent for histopathology. Postoperative condition was stable. Patient was transferred to [[BED_NUMBER|12]] in [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]] for observation. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-JMH-46-F-001]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Rameshbhai Makvana]] was informed of the procedure and postoperative status. MRN: [[MRN|MRN-JMH-2024-8891]].

persona: Narendra Rathinam blends a mason’s grit with an artist’s eye, balancing hard‑working ambition, rhythmic devotion, and a love for community traditions; they navigate life with curiosity and a touch of practical ‘jugaad’.
cultural_background: Narendra hails from a rural community near Madurai in Tamil Nadu. He grew up in a traditional Tamil household where family ties, temple festivals, and agrarian rhythms shape daily life. The local culture blends Tamil folk arts, such as karagattam and bharatanatyam, with the practical ethos of construction work that many villagers take up. While primarily Tamil, he is also exposed to Telugu customs through neighboring communities, giving him a modest cross‑cultural familiarity.

His formal schooling only up to matric provides him basic literacy in Hindi and English, sufficient for reading work orders, safety signs, and simple communication but not advanced fluency.
skills_and_expertise: Narendra has hands‑on experience in masonry, bricklaying, concrete mixing, and basic carpentry, honed from years of working on residential and small commercial projects. He is adept at reading construction drawings at a practical level, measuring dimensions accurately, and operating hand tools such as trowels, hammers, and power drills. His work also includes site safety awareness, teamwork, and problem‑solving using low‑cost ‘jugaad’ methods. Physical stamina and reliability in demanding schedules are notable strengths.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Prakasam.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Telugu script for Telugu (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Puncher, Metal
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Prakasam
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32-year-old male metal puncher from Prakasam district, Andhra Pradesh, who is currently married, has an illiterate education, and speaks Telugu as his first language. He works in a rural setting and follows a routine that includes morning walks and folk songs. His lifestyle is characterized by a focus on work, family savings, and occasional back strain.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramu]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Male]] సర్జన్ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Venkateswara Rao]]
ప్రక్రియ / ఫలితాలు: రోగి గత మూడు రోజులుగా తీవ్రమైన ఆందోళన మరియు కుటుంబ సభ్యుల పట్ల హింసాత్మక ప్రవర్తనతో వచ్చారు. ప్రాథమిక అంచనా తీవ్రమైన ఆందోళన, నిద్రలేమి మరియు శ్రవణ భ్రాంతులను (auditory hallucinations) వెల్లడించింది. రోగి తనకే మరియు ఇతరులకు ప్రమాదకరమని భావించబడింది, కాబట్టి అత్యవసర జోక్యం అవసరమైంది. అతని భార్య [[RELATIVE_NAME|Lakshmi]] నుండి సమాచారంతో కూడిన సమ్మతి పొందిన తర్వాత, రోగి ఎలక్ట్రోకన్వల్సివ్ థెరపీ (ECT) కోసం సిద్ధం చేయబడ్డారు. సాధారణ అనస్థీషియా కింద ప్రక్రియ నిర్వహించబడింది మరియు సుమారు 45 సెకన్ల పాటు మూర్ఛ (seizure) ప్రేరేపించబడింది. ప్రక్రియ తర్వాత, రోగిని రికవరీ ఏరియాలో రెండు గంటల పాటు పర్యవేక్షించారు, ఆపై వార్డుకు తిరిగి పంపబడ్డారు. ECT తర్వాత రోగి మానసిక స్థితి గందరగోళంగా ఉన్నప్పటికీ స్థిరంగా ఉంది. ప్రతికూల సంఘటనలు ఏవీ గమనించబడలేదు. రోగి భార్యకు చికిత్స ప్రణాళిక మరియు తదుపరి షెడ్యూల్ గురించి కౌన్సెలింగ్ ఇవ్వబడింది. రోగి యొక్క [[MRN|MRN-2024-1123-001]] ప్రక్రియ వివరాలతో నవీకరించబడింది. పరిశీలన కోసం అతనికి [[BED_NUMBER|B2-08]] కేటాయించబడింది. అతని [[PATIENT_ID|PID-PRK-2024-0032]] ఈ ఎన్‌కౌంటర్ [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-1123-01]] తో అనుసంధానించబడింది. రోగికి వారానికి మూడు సార్లు ఆరు ECT సెషన్ల కోర్సు షెడ్యూల్ చేయబడింది. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-AP-045]] ప్రోటోకాల్ ECT అనుసరించబడింది. తదుపరి కమ్యూనికేషన్ కోసం రోగి యొక్క [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] రికార్డ్ చేయబడింది. రోగి [[VILLAGE|Chandrasekharapuram]], [[DISTRICT|Prakasam]] లో నివసిస్తున్నారు.

Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government General Hospital, Ongole]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1123]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramu]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Venkateswara Rao]]
Procedure / findings: The patient presented with a history of acute agitation and violent behavior towards family members over the past three days. Initial assessment revealed severe anxiety, insomnia, and auditory hallucinations. The patient was deemed a danger to self and others, necessitating urgent intervention. After obtaining informed consent from his wife, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Lakshmi]], the patient was prepared for electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). The procedure was performed under general anesthesia with a brief seizure induced, lasting approximately 45 seconds. Post-procedure, the patient was monitored in the recovery area for two hours and then transferred back to the ward. The patient's immediate post-ECT mental status was confused but stabilized. The patient was observed for any adverse events, which were not noted. The patient's wife was counseled on the treatment plan and follow-up schedule. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-1123-001]] was updated with the procedure details. He was assigned [[BED_NUMBER|B2-08]] for observation. His [[PATIENT_ID|PID-PRK-2024-0032]] is linked to this encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-1123-01]]. The patient is scheduled for a course of six ECT sessions, three times a week. The [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-AP-045]] protocol for ECT was followed. The patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] was recorded for follow-up communication. The patient resides in [[VILLAGE|Chandrasekharapuram]], [[DISTRICT|Prakasam]].
judge_reasoning: Telugu prose with allowed entity tags; ECT note fits 32yo male psychiatric case; no script/domain/persona violations or invented types.

professional_persona: Mohan Narayanan, a retired household manager, channels his meticulous budgeting, event coordination, and culinary expertise into mentoring neighbourhood youth on financial prudence and cultural literacy, and they plan a modest home‑based pickle venture with careful notebooks.
linguistic_persona: Mohan Narayanan fluently reads and writes Urdu, converses comfortably in Tamil, and handles basic English newspaper reading and digital communication, and they seamlessly switch scripts to connect with their community.
sports_persona: Mohan Narayanan enjoys watching cricket, especially cheering for the Chennai Super Kings, plays casual chess and rummy with friends, and goes for early morning walks around his neighbourhood park to stay fit, and they prefer low‑impact activities over high‑intensity sports.
arts_persona: Mohan Narayanan recites Ghalib and Faiz at family gatherings, listens avidly to Carnatic ragas, occasionally plays the harmonium, admires classic Tamil cinema icons like Sivaji Ganesan, and they practice Urdu calligraphy as a meditative art.
travel_persona: Mohan Narayanan prefers pilgrimages to sacred Vaishnavite sites like Tirupati and Venkateswara, enjoys weekend getaways to Ooty’s tea estates, and dreams of exploring Delhi’s historic monuments to connect with his Urdu roots, and they plan trips with careful budgeting and a modest travel itinerary.
culinary_persona: Mohan Narayanan loves the aromatic tang of sambar, the crispness of idli, and the fragrant layers of biryani, enjoys savoring mango pickles and coriander chutney, but rarely cooks for himself, preferring to order street‑food chaat on busy evenings, and they occasionally experiment with homemade chutney for the pickle business.
persona: Mohan Narayanan blends Urdu poetry with Tamil traditions, budgets meticulously for his pickle dreams, and despite a reserved nature, they chat about cricket scores over evening tea.
cultural_background: Mohan grew up in the bustling urban neighbourhood of Salem, Tamil Nadu, where the streets echo with Tamil chatter, but his home resonated with Urdu poetry and North‑Indian flavours. As a Hindu family that migrated from the Uttar‑Pradesh region in the early 1970s, his cultural tapestry weaves together Tamil festivals like Pongal with Urdu literary gatherings, creating a hybrid identity that respects both the Tamil and Urdu heritage while rooted firmly in Hindu rituals.
linguistic_background: Mohan’s mother tongue is Urdu, which he reads and writes fluently thanks to early exposure at home and community gatherings. He acquired Tamil as his second language during school, achieving functional literacy and comfortable conversational skills that allow him to navigate daily life in Salem. His formal education ended at the secondary level, so his English proficiency is modest, limited to basic reading of newspapers and occasional correspondence. He is confident in both Urdu and Tamil scripts, and can switch seamlessly between languages in social settings.
skills_and_expertise: Mohan possesses practical household management skills honed over years of running his family home. He is adept at budgeting, planning daily chores, and coordinating family events. His culinary expertise spans traditional Tamil dishes such as idli, sambar, and biryani influenced by North‑Indian flavours. He maintains a modest vegetable garden, applying basic horticultural techniques. He is digitally literate, comfortable using a smartphone for WhatsApp, video calls, and online banking. His Urdu proficiency enables him to read classical poetry and translate religious verses. He also volunteers occasionally as a coordinator for temple festivals, showcasing organizational and leadership abilities.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Salem.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Arabic script for Urdu (not English; not a different Indic script).

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 48
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Tamil Nadu
- district: Salem
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Mohan Narayanan, a 48-year-old retired male from Salem, Tamil Nadu, is currently married and has a matriculation education. He resides in an urban zone and speaks Urdu as his first language, with Tamil as a second language. His lifestyle includes meticulous budgeting and a preference for low-impact activities like morning walks.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|Mohan Narayanan]]: آپ کا آنکولوجی فالو اپ [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-ONC-240521-01]] ہے [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Salem]] میں 21-مئی کو صبح 10:30 بجے [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]] کے ساتھ۔ براہ کرم [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] کے ذریعے تصدیق کریں۔ MRN [[MRN|MRN-TN-2024-0815]]۔

Please confirm via [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-TN-2024-0815]].
